text: Welcome back. You just watched two rocks pull each other across empty space.
visual: Two grey rocks float in deep black space, slowly drifting toward each other — a direct echo of the gravity lesson's opening shot.

text: Now the rocks are yours.
visual: The rocks settle to a stop. The deep cosmic black around them gently tightens into a rectangular frame — becoming the canvas of the homework interface.

text: Two sliders for mass. One for distance. A play button. A timer.
visual: Below the canvas, the control panel materializes piece by piece: two horizontal sliders labeled 'Mass A' and 'Mass B,' a third labeled 'Distance,' a white 'Play' button, and a timer reading '0.0 seconds.' Each element appears in turn as it's named.

text: One instruction. Make them collide in exactly sixty seconds.
visual: A line of text fades in above the canvas: 'Make these two rocks collide in exactly 60 seconds.' The '60' glows briefly.

text: Looks simple. It isn't.
visual: The whole interface holds still. A faint, almost shy question mark hovers off to one side, not quite intruding.

text: Because your real job isn't to make them collide in sixty seconds.
visual: The '60' in the instruction line dims slightly, sliding gently out of focus.

text: Your real job is to predict what your rocks are going to do — before you press play.
visual: A text box slides into view below the canvas. A label fades in across it: 'Before you press play.' The Play button stays dim in the background.

text: How many seconds until they meet. And why.
visual: The text box fills out: 'With mass A = 10, mass B = 10, distance = 400, I predict they will collide in __ seconds, because __.' The two blank fields pulse gently.

text: Then you press play. And you watch.
visual: The Play button brightens. The two rocks begin to drift toward each other. The timer ticks upward: 12, 25, 38.

text: After — you explain the gap.
visual: The rocks meet softly. A reflection text box slides into view, showing 'predicted: ___ seconds, actual: ___ seconds, gap: ___.'

text: If you said thirty and they collided in fifty — what did you miss? Mass? Distance? Both?
visual: Three small icons appear in sequence beside the gap: a weight (mass), a ruler (distance), and both together. Each pulses briefly as it's named.

text: You get three tries. That's all.
visual: Three small counter dots appear in the corner: ● ○ ○. A label beneath them reads 'Attempts.'

text: So don't burn them by clicking around. Random clicking is the trap.
visual: A cursor zips back and forth across the sliders rapidly, jittering. A soft red X overlays the motion. Then everything stills.

text: The trick is to think first. Then press play.
visual: A cursor hovers calmly over the Play button without pressing it. A small thought bubble forms above the canvas, holding empty for a moment.

text: Your first prediction will probably be wrong. That's not a problem. That's the point.
visual: Split frame. Left: 'Predicted: 30s.' Right: 'Actual: 75s.' A neutral marker — neither red nor green, just a quiet checkmark — appears between them.

text: Wrong is where the learning lives. Each miss tells you which lever you misjudged.
visual: Three rows of 'predicted vs actual' appear in sequence. Row one: gap of 45 seconds. Row two: gap of 15 seconds. Row three: gap of 2 seconds. The gap visibly shrinks down the column.

text: By the third try, your prediction will be close.
visual: Hold on the third row of the table. 'Predicted: 58s, actual: 60s.' A soft glow surrounds the numbers.

text: Not because you memorized anything. Because you built a feeling for what gravity does.
visual: Two side-by-side icons: an equation symbol on the left, faded and gray; a hand resting on a slider on the right, brightening. The hand icon glows softly.

text: And after the three tries — one last thing.
visual: All three attempt dots are now filled: ● ● ●. The control panel quietly dims. A new text box begins to slide into focus from below.

text: Write down, in your own words, what you understand about gravity now that you didn't understand before.
visual: The final reflection text box fills the center of the frame. The prompt is clear: 'In your own words — what did you learn about gravity that you didn't know before?' A cursor blinks in the empty field.

text: No right answer. Just what you saw.
visual: Hold on the empty field. The cursor blinks steadily. The rest of the page softens into dim focus around it.

text: That sentence is the real homework. Everything else was practice for it.
visual: The cursor blinks once more. The full homework page comes back into soft focus behind the text box, with the canvas, sliders, and Play button all visible but quiet.

text: So. Look at the sliders. Look at the rocks. Imagine what they're about to do.
visual: The homework interface returns to full clarity. The two rocks sit at their starting positions. The sliders are at their defaults. The Play button waits, untouched.

text: Then write it down. And see how close you got.
visual: A cursor reaches into the prediction text box. A character or two begin to appear in the empty field, just enough to show the act of writing has started.

text: Welcome to running your own universe.
visual: Pull back to show the full interface, alive and ready. The two rocks float in their starting positions. The cursor blinks once on the prediction field. Hold. Fade.
